Code Item Severity TACHYON Application Check Method WEB-20 Enable SSL/TLS High HTTPS required Nginx SSL settings, TLS 1.2+ enforcement, certificate validity check WEB-21 HTTP Redirection Medium Force HTTP to HTTPS Verify 80 to 443 redirect WEB-22 Error Page Management Low Custom error pages Prevent stack trace/path exposure WEB-23 LDAP Authentication Algorithm Configuration Medium TACHYON Auth integration Check SSL/TLS usage for LDAP binding. N/A if TACHYON does not use LDAP WEB-24 Upload Path Separation and Permission Settings Medium Support bundle upload Verify upload directory is located outside web root with no execution permissions. tkadmin: /usr/local/tkadmin/cache/

Code Item Severity TACHYON Application Check Method WEB-25 Periodic Security Patching High Nginx/Go runtime patches Check versions with nginx -v, go version WEB-26 Log Directory and File Permissions Medium Access log permissions Verify access.log, error.log are 640 or below

Code Item N/A Rationale WEB-05 CGI Execution Restriction Go native server, CGI not in use WEB-15 Unnecessary Script Mapping Only Nginx reverse proxy is used, no script mapping WEB-18 Disable WebDAV Disabled by default in Nginx WEB-19 Restrict SSI Usage Server-Side Includes not in use
Reference: KISA 2026 Guide Chapter 8 -- DBMS Vulnerability Analysis & Assessment Target: TACHYON MariaDB (MySQL compatible)

Code Item Severity TACHYON Application Check Method D-01 Change Default Account Password High MariaDB root password mysql -u root -p login test D-02 Remove/Lock Unnecessary Accounts High test accounts, empty password accounts SELECT user, host FROM mysql.user D-03 Password Complexity/Usage Period High validate_password plugin SHOW VARIABLES LIKE 'validate_password%' D-04 Minimize Administrator Privilege Grants High SUPER privilege restriction SELECT user, Super_priv FROM mysql.user D-06 Individual User Account Assignment Medium Prohibit shared accounts Check if same username is used with multiple hosts D-07 Restrict Root Privilege Service Execution Medium mysqld execution user ps -ef | grep mysqld -- verify user=mysql D-08 Secure Encryption Algorithm High Authentication plugin Verify caching_sha2_password or ed25519 usage D-09 Login Failure Lockout Policy Medium FAILED_LOGIN_ATTEMPTS ALTER USER ... FAILED_LOGIN_ATTEMPTS 5

Code Item Severity TACHYON Application Check Method D-10 Restrict Remote DB Access High host != '%' SELECT user, host FROM mysql.user WHERE host='%' D-11 Restrict System Table Access High mysql.* access restriction Check regular user access to mysql DB D-14 Key Configuration File Permissions Medium my.cnf 600/640 ls -la /etc/my.cnf* permission check

Code Item Severity TACHYON Application Check Method D-18 Public Role Restriction Medium Ensure application/DBA account roles are not set to Public SELECT user, host FROM mysql.user -- check for unnecessary global privileges (*.*). MariaDB: SHOW GRANTS FOR 'user'@'host' D-20 Object Owner Restriction Medium Prevent unauthorized users from owning DB/tables SELECT table_schema, table_name FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema NOT IN ('mysql','information_schema','performance_schema','sys') -- check for unauthorized schema ownership D-21 GRANT OPTION Usage Restriction Medium Prevent privilege delegation by regular users SELECT user, Grant_priv FROM mysql.user

Code Item Severity TACHYON Application Check Method D-25 Periodic Security Patching High MariaDB version SELECT VERSION() -- verify latest stable version D-26 Audit Record Policy High Enable audit logging Check general_log, slow_query_log settings

Code Item N/A Rationale D-05 Password Reuse Restriction password_history not supported in MariaDB (MySQL 8.0+ only) D-12 Oracle Listener Security Oracle only D-13 Windows ODBC Data Source Windows only D-15 Oracle Listener Logging Oracle only D-16 MSSQL Windows Authentication Mode MSSQL only D-17 Oracle Audit Table Oracle only D-19 Oracle OS_ROLES Oracle only D-22 Oracle RESOURCE_LIMIT Oracle only D-23 MSSQL xp_cmdshell MSSQL only D-24 MSSQL Registry Procedure MSSQL only
documents.s: 6.5 N/A Items (Not Applicable to TACHYON MariaDB)

documents.t: TACHYON Component Check Domain Related KISA Items tkadmin (Go Server) Web Services WEB-0103, WEB-09, WEB-1314, WEB-20~22 Nginx (Reverse Proxy) Web Services WEB-04, WEB-06, WEB-08, WEB-10, WEB-12, WEB-16, WEB-25~26 MariaDB DBMS D-0104, D-0611, D-14, D-21, D-25~26 OpenSearch Service Management U-27 (Connection IP/Port Restriction), WEB-20 (TLS) Kafka Service Management U-27, U-52 (Unnecessary Services), WEB-13 (Config Exposure) Redis Service Management U-27, D-01 (Default Password), D-10 (Remote Access Restriction) OS (Rocky Linux) Unix Server All U-01~67

Priority Scope Item Count Description P0 U-01~U-13 (Account) 13 Account/Password Security -- Immediate Risk P1 U-14~U-35 (File) + WEB-03,13 24 File Permissions + Config Exposure P2 D-01~D-11 (DBMS Account/Access) 11 MariaDB Security P3 WEB-04~WEB-26 (Web) 17 Nginx + Web Service Security

This document defines the security scan and remediation logic that tkctl will perform at a production-grade level of detail, based on the Detailed Guide for Technical Vulnerability Analysis and Assessment of Critical Information and Communication Infrastructure.
documents.s: KISA Vulnerability Analysis & Assessment Implementation Specification (2026)

documents.t: The security module is designed with an interface-based architecture for extensibility. All scan results must be serializable to JSON.

package security // CheckStatus - Scan status constants type CheckStatus string const ( StatusGood CheckStatus = "GOOD" // Compliant StatusBad CheckStatus = "BAD" // Vulnerable StatusNA CheckStatus = "NA" // Not Applicable (service not in use, etc.) StatusError CheckStatus = "ERROR" // Check failed (insufficient permissions, etc.) ) // SecurityItem - Scan item metadata type SecurityItem struct { Code string // e.g., "U-01" Category string // "Linux", "DBMS", "Web" Level string // "High", "Medium", "Low" Name string // Item name (e.g., "Restrict remote access to root account") Description string // Detailed description Criteria string // Assessment criteria Guide string // Remediation guide (text) } // CheckResult - Scan result type CheckResult struct { Item SecurityItem Status CheckStatus CurrentVal string // Current state (e.g., "PermitRootLogin yes") Message string // Detailed message Fixed bool // Whether remediation was performed FixLog string // Remediation result log } // Scanner interface type Scanner interface { Name() string Detect(ctx context.Context) ([]CheckResult, error) Fix(ctx context.Context, itemCode string) (bool, error) }

Target: RHEL/CentOS/Rocky, Ubuntu/Debian families Code Item Name Severity Check Details Fix Logic U-01 Restrict remote access to root account High Whether SSH root login is allowed sed -i 's/^PermitRootLogin yes/PermitRootLogin no/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config then reload U-02 Password complexity settings High Combination of letters/numbers/special characters and length Inject settings such as minlen=8, lcredit=-1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf U-03 Account lockout threshold settings High Account lockout after failed logins (5 attempts) Check and add pam_faillock.so or pam_tally2.so configuration U-04 Password file protection High /etc/passwd permission check chmod 644 /etc/passwd && chown root:root /etc/passwd U-05 root home/path directory permissions High Whether PATH contains . Guide to remove . from PATH variable in user .bash_profile, etc. (auto-fix risky) U-06 File/directory owner settings High Existence of files without owner (nouser) Scan with find / -nouser -o -nogroup and report (manual deletion recommended) U-07 passwd file owner/permissions High /etc/passwd permissions Same as U-04 (duplicate item, separate check) U-08 shadow file owner/permissions High /etc/shadow permissions chmod 400 /etc/shadow && chown root:root /etc/shadow U-09 hosts file owner/permissions High /etc/hosts permissions chmod 600 /etc/hosts && chown root:root /etc/hosts U-10 xinetd.conf file permissions High /etc/xinetd.conf permissions chmod 600 /etc/xinetd.conf (if file exists) U-11 rsyslog.conf file permissions High /etc/rsyslog.conf permissions chmod 640 /etc/rsyslog.conf U-12 services file permissions High /etc/services permissions chmod 644 /etc/services U-13 SUID, SGID configured files High Unnecessary SetUID files Provide guide to remove SUID (chmod -s) for non-essential files U-14 User startup file permissions High .bashrc etc. permissions Guide to set home directory config file permissions to 640 or below U-15 World Writable files High Files writable by anyone Scan with find / -type f -perm -2 and report U-16 Directories without device files High Unnecessary files in /dev Scan for regular files within /dev directory U-17 $HOME/.rhosts permissions High r-command authentication file Recommend file deletion (rm .rhosts) U-18 Connection IP and port restriction High hosts.allow/deny settings Check if ALL:ALL is set in /etc/hosts.deny U-19 Disable Finger service High Whether Finger service is running systemctl disable --now finger U-20 Disable Anonymous FTP High Anonymous FTP access allowed Set anonymous_enable=NO in vsftpd.conf U-21 Disable r-series services High rlogin, rsh, rexec Stop related services with systemctl disable --now rlogin, etc. U-22 Cron file owner/permissions High /etc/crontab etc. permissions chmod 640 /etc/crontab U-23 Services vulnerable to DoS attacks High echo, discard, etc. Set disable = yes for those services in xinetd configuration U-24 Disable NFS service High Unnecessary NFS running Stop with systemctl stop nfs if not in use U-25 NFS access control High /etc/exports settings Vulnerable if * (allow all) setting exists U-26 Disable automountd High Auto-mount service systemctl disable --now autofs U-27 RPC service check High Unnecessary RPC services Analyze rpcinfo -p results U-28 NIS, NIS+ check High NIS service usage systemctl disable --now ypserv U-29 Disable tftp, talk services High Vulnerable service status systemctl disable --now tftp U-30 Sendmail version check High Whether latest version is used Check version via package manager

Connection method: Connect to local/remote DB as root account and execute SQL queries. Code Item Name Severity Check Query / Logic Fix Guide D-01 Change default account password High Check if root password is NULL or default Provide ALTER USER statement D-02 Remove unnecessary accounts Medium Check for default accounts such as test, guest DROP USER 'test'@'%' D-03 Restrict remote access High Check bind-address and accounts with Host='%' Local binding and host privilege reduction D-04 Password policy settings High validate_password plugin configuration values Guide for INSTALL PLUGIN validate_password D-05 Login failure lockout High Check connection_control plugin Plugin activation and threshold configuration D-06 File system access restriction High Check local_infile variable SET GLOBAL local_infile = 0 D-07 Enable audit logging High Check general_log or Audit plugin Enable audit logging (my.cnf) D-08 Separation of administrator privileges High Check non-admin accounts with SUPER privilege Revoke unnecessary admin privileges (REVOKE) D-09 Session timeout settings Medium interactive_timeout, wait_timeout values Recommend timeout value of 600 seconds (10 minutes) or less D-10 Apply latest security patches High Compare SELECT VERSION() results Recommend updating to latest minor version

Target: Main configuration (nginx.conf) and sites-enabled/*.conf parsing. Code Item Name Severity Check Logic Fix Configuration N-01 Remove directory listing High Detect autoindex on; directive Change to or delete autoindex off; N-02 Restrict unnecessary methods High Check for missing limit_except block Add limit_except GET POST { deny all; } N-03 Restrict admin page access High Check ACL for paths like /admin, /manager Set allow 127.0.0.1; deny all; N-04 Restrict hidden file access High Check for location ~ /\. { deny all; } Add the location block N-05 Remove unnecessary files High Search for .bak, .old, .log files in web root Recommend moving backup files outside web root N-06 Hide web service information High Check for server_tokens on; Set server_tokens off; N-07 Error page configuration Medium Check default error page exposure Connect custom pages via error_page directive N-08 File upload size limit High Check client_max_body_size value Set appropriate size limit (e.g., 10M) N-09 Web process privileges High Check if user directive is root Run as www-data or nginx account N-10 HTTPS encrypted communication High Check SSL/TLS configuration (listen 443 ssl) Recommend applying certificate and redirecting port 80

OS/Service Detection: Detect whether the current system is Linux and which services (MariaDB, Nginx) are running. Parallel Scanning: When possible, perform parallel scans by category using goroutines. Result Aggregation: Collect all results into a list of CheckResult structs. Report Generation: Save results as a JSON file (scan_report_YYYYMMDD.json).

Backup: Must perform cp source dest.bak before modifying configuration files. Rollback: If service restart fails after remediation, implement automatic restoration from backup file (Rollback() method).

tkctl analyze security # Full scan tkctl analyze security --target os # OS (Linux) scan only tkctl analyze security --target db # DB scan only tkctl fix security --code U-01 # Fix specific item tkctl fix security --auto # Fix all auto-remediable items (use with caution) This specification is designed to meet the robustness of a production environment and the KISA certification standards.

This appendix describes in detail the configuration file locations and parsing logic that the tkctl service loglevel command references to diagnose each service's logging level. System engineers can refer to this guide to understand the basis of information reported by tkctl and manually verify settings when necessary.
documents.s: Appendix A: Service Logging Level Diagnostic Technical Specification

documents.t: tkctl directly parses each service's configuration files as text or uses a YAML parser to extract logging-related directives. Each service searches for relative paths based on the installation base directory (${BaseDir}).

Service Reference File Path Diagnostic Method (Parsing Logic) NGINX nginx/conf/nginx.conf 1. Error Log: Extracts the last argument of the error_log directive to display the level (DEBUG, INFO, etc.). 2. Access Log: Since NGINX does not use a level concept for access logs, it only checks for the presence of the access_log off; directive and reports as ON/OFF status. Display format: Level (Access: ON/OFF) Redis redis/conf/redis.conf or redis/redis.conf Extracts the right-side value of the loglevel directive. Display example: loglevel notice → NOTICE MariaDB mariadb/my.cnf Only checks file existence and displays the default value INFO (Default). (MariaDB primarily uses detailed logging settings rather than a single level setting)
All TACHYON Java services (api, auth, manager, stat, batch, report, watchdog) search for files according to the following priority.

YAML Parsing (.yml, .yml_dev)​ Uses the gopkg.in/yaml.v3 library to extract values from the following hierarchy: Path: logging > level > root Structure example: logging: level: root: INFO # <-- extraction target XML Parsing (.xml)​ Extracts the level attribute of the <root> tag through string pattern matching: Regex/Pattern: <root level="([^"]+)"> Structure example: <root level="DEBUG"> <!-- <-- level attribute value extracted --> <appender-ref ref="CONSOLE" /> </root>

Service Reference File Path Diagnostic Method (Parsing Logic) tkadmin ${BaseDir}/tkadmin.yml Extracts the logging.level.root value through YAML parsing. tkctl ${BaseDir}/tkctl.ini Checks the log_level key value in the configuration file. (INFO if not set)

Status Meaning Recommended Action OK Configuration file found and parsed successfully - CONFIG NOT FOUND Configuration file does not exist at the specified path Verify the installation path of the service. INVALID File was found but logging level directive is missing Check whether the root level setting is missing in the configuration file. - Diagnosis not possible Specify a particular service for detailed diagnosis. Note: For Java services, tkctl recommends a management workflow of modifying the yml_dev file and copying it to yml for application.
The tkctl env command diagnoses and optimizes kernel and system environment variables essential for TACHYON operations.
documents.s: 6. System Environment Configuration Diagnostic and Setup Specification (env)

documents.t: Category Diagnostic/Reference Logic Current execution mode Prioritizes executing the getenforce command; if unavailable, extracts Current mode from sestatus output. Permanent configuration mode Directly parses the SELINUX= directive value from /etc/selinux/config file. Change process When --set is executed, performs both immediate application via setenforce and permanent application via config modification simultaneously. (Provides reboot guidance when setting to Disabled)

Category Diagnostic/Reference Logic Kernel-wide limits Parses the /etc/security/limits.conf file to collect nofile settings by domain. Per-service limits Queries the actual values assigned at startup through systemctl show -p LimitNOFILE --value [SVC] for systemd units. TACHYON block When configuring via the --set feature, manages a dedicated block using # --- TACHYON LIMITS START --- markers to prevent corruption of existing files.

Active status detection: Simultaneously checks whether firewalld and iptables services are running via systemctl is-active. Port list extraction: When firewalld is active, parses and provides results from firewall-cmd --list-ports and --list-services. Auto-allow logic: When the --set argument is omitted, automatically detects and opens the TACHYON management port (443) and the currently active SSH port.

Port detection: Searches for uncommented Port directives in the /etc/ssh/sshd_config file. (Assumes standard port 22 if directive is absent) Change and integration workflow: Modify the Port number in /etc/ssh/sshd_config Permanently allow the new port through the firewall via firewall-cmd --add-port=[NEW_PORT]/tcp (--permanent) Execute firewall-cmd --reload to immediately apply firewall settings Execute systemctl restart sshd to complete the configuration
The tkctl service jvm command follows this technical specification to diagnose and optimize heap memory for JVM-based services.
documents.s: 7. JVM Memory Configuration and Auto-Optimization Technical Specification (service jvm)

documents.t: Service Configuration File Path Extraction/Modification Logic OpenSearch opensearch/opensearch/config/jvm.options Directly parses lines starting with -Xms, -Xmx. Kafka kafka/bin/kafka-server-start.sh Extracts the KAFKA_HEAP_OPTS environment variable assignment line using regex. Logstash logstash/logstash-kafka-os/config/jvm.options Directly parses -Xms, -Xmx lines. TACHYON Services [svc]/bin/tachyon-[svc].sh Extracts -Xms, -Xmx patterns from java command arguments in the execution script. Watchdog bin/tachyon-watchdog.sh Uses the same shell script regex parsing method.
documents.s: 7.1 Per-Service Configuration Files and Parsing Logic

documents.t: When the --set auto option is executed, system total memory is allocated based on the following weight model. Optimization formula: Allocation = (Total Memory - Base OS Occupancy) * Per-Service Weight Per-service weights: opensearch: 20% kafka: 10% api: 5% manager, auth, stat, batch, report: 3~5% each watchdog: 2% (minimum 128MB guaranteed) Guardrails: Maximum cap: Single service heap size is capped at 16GB. Minimum floor: Minimum floors of 256MB~1GB (varies by service) are applied to ensure service visibility. Agent count weighting: Higher --agents values dynamically increase the proportion of API and Manager services.

Backup policy: Creates a ${ConfigFile}.bak file before changing settings. If a backup already exists, it uses timestamps to protect the history instead of overwriting. Resource warning: If total JVM allocation exceeds 80% of physical memory, a warning message is output and manual review is recommended for operational stability.
This section provides the technical basis for the phenomenon where memory usage reported by the tkctl info command appears higher than what users perceive.
documents.s: 8. OS Memory Management and Occupancy Technical Specification (Memory Management)

documents.t: Item Linux (Operational Environment) Windows (Comparison Environment) Core philosophy "Free RAM is wasted RAM" User-visible resource-oriented flexible allocation Cache utilization Dedicates all remaining memory to Page Cache for disk I/O performance improvement Performs prefetching via Superfetch, but tends to count as 'available' in reporting Usage calculation Used = Total - Free (Free is purely unused physical space) Used = Total - Available (includes immediately available cache as available)
documents.s: 8.1 Linux vs Windows Memory Management Philosophy Differences

documents.t: Over-occupancy phenomenon (Memory Occupancy): The Linux kernel retains file data in physical memory (Page Cache) when reading or writing files. This allows subsequent access to the same file to respond immediately from fast memory instead of slow disk. TACHYON services (OpenSearch, MariaDB, etc.) continuously process large amounts of data, so cache occupancy increases over time and Free memory may display as close to 0. User visibility: tkctl info directly reports the physical occupancy state of the OS kernel, so it includes all cache areas as 'in use'. Actual availability (Available Memory): This cache area is reclaimable space that the OS can immediately free when an application requests actual memory allocation. Therefore, even if the occupancy percentage is high, it does not affect the system's practical work capacity.

The high memory occupancy shown in tkctl info is mostly the normal result of Linux kernel optimization for I/O performance improvement. To determine whether system resources are actually insufficient, use the following indicators rather than occupancy percentages. Swap occurrence: Check whether physical memory shortage is causing frequent disk compression/swapping. OOM Killer logs: Check kernel logs (dmesg) for process forced termination history due to memory exhaustion. TIP: To know the practically 'available' amount in a Linux environment, refer to the Available metric rather than the Free (idle) metric. (tkctl plans to include the available metric in future versions.)

This appendix describes in detail the internal operating principles and query structures used by the tkctl analyze command to analyze log data from MariaDB and OpenSearch. System engineers can refer to this guide to verify tkctl analysis results or perform manual analysis directly without the CLI tool.

The tkctl analyze command queries two data sources from the TACHYON system: Data Source Purpose Connection Information Source OpenSearch Real-time log aggregation, anomaly detection app_info.properties → opensearch_ip, opensearch_port, opensearch_id, opensearch_pw MariaDB Log table growth trend analysis app_info.properties → db_ip, db_port, db_user, db_password

The TACHYON system stores media control event logs in monthly indices in OpenSearch. Index name format: {SPCODE}_{YEAR}_{MONTH} Examples: Organization code (SPCODE): 00133fkfkg January 2026: 00133fkfkg_2026_01 December 2025: 00133fkfkg_2025_12 How to check SPCODE: # Check from app_info.properties file grep "spcode" /usr/local/TACHYON/TTS40/conf/app_info.properties_dev # Or check database name (same value) tkctl info Manual index listing: # Query directly via OpenSearch API curl -s "http://localhost:9200/_cat/indices?v" | grep -E "00133fkfkg_"

Main field structure of the media control log index: Field Name Type Description Example Value server_dt keyword Server reception date (YYYY-MM-DD) 2026-01-15 client_dt keyword Client event occurrence date 2026-01-15 puid keyword Agent unique identifier (UUID) a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890 process_nm keyword/text Execution process path C:\Windows\Explorer.EXE file_nm keyword/text Target file/path accessed E:\Documents\report.xlsx event_type keyword Event type code READ, WRITE, COPY Note: Fields with the .keyword suffix are used for exact match searches and aggregations. In aggregation queries, be sure to use the process_nm.keyword form.

Purpose: Identify which processes accessed which paths most frequently 9.4.1 OpenSearch DSL Query​ When executing tkctl analyze media-top --month 2026-01 --limit 20, the following DSL is used internally: { "size": 0, "query": { "range": { "server_dt": { "gte": "2026-01-01", "lt": "2026-02-01" } } }, "aggs": { "by_process_path": { "composite": { "size": 20, "sources": [ { "process": { "terms": { "field": "process_nm.keyword" } } }, { "path": { "terms": { "field": "file_nm.keyword" } } } ] } } } } 9.4.2 Manual Execution Method​ # Execute query directly on OpenSearch curl -X POST "http://localhost:9200/00133fkfkg_2026_01/_search" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -u "admin:admin" \ -d '{ "size": 0, "query": { "range": { "server_dt": { "gte": "2026-01-01", "lt": "2026-02-01" } } }, "aggs": { "by_process_path": { "composite": { "size": 10, "sources": [ { "process": { "terms": { "field": "process_nm.keyword" } } }, { "path": { "terms": { "field": "file_nm.keyword" } } } ] } } } }' | jq '.aggregations.by_process_path.buckets' 9.4.3 Response Structure and Interpretation​ { "buckets": [ { "key": { "process": "C:\\Windows\\Explorer.EXE", "path": "" }, "doc_count": 42349 }, { "key": { "process": "C:\\Windows\\system32\\svchost.exe", "path": "" }, "doc_count": 5966 } ] } key.process: Execution process path key.path: Target file/path accessed (empty string indicates events without a specified path) doc_count: Number of occurrences for the combination
documents.s: 9.4 media-top: Top N Analysis by Process+Path

documents.t: Purpose: Identify agents logging excessively compared to the average 9.5.1 OpenSearch DSL Query​ { "size": 0, "query": { "range": { "server_dt": { "gte": "2026-01-01", "lt": "2026-02-01" } } }, "aggs": { "by_agent": { "terms": { "field": "puid.keyword", "size": 10000 } }, "agent_stats": { "extended_stats_bucket": { "buckets_path": "by_agent._count" } } } } 9.5.2 Key Aggregation Description​ Aggregation Type Field Description terms by_agent Log count aggregation per agent (PUID) extended_stats_bucket agent_stats Bucket statistics calculation (mean, standard deviation, etc.) 9.5.3 Anomaly Detection Algorithm​ tkctl uses the following logic to determine anomalous agents: Anomaly = (Agent Log Count / Overall Average) >= Threshold (default 5.0) Assessment criteria: Critical: 10x or more above average (Multiplier >= 10.0) Warning: 5x to 10x above average (Multiplier >= 5.0) 9.5.4 Manual Analysis Procedure​ # 1. Query per-agent statistics curl -X POST "http://localhost:9200/00133fkfkg_2026_01/_search" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -u "admin:admin" \ -d '{ "size": 0, "aggs": { "by_agent": { "terms": { "field": "puid.keyword", "size": 10000 } }, "agent_stats": { "extended_stats_bucket": { "buckets_path": "by_agent._count" } } } }' > /tmp/agent_stats.json # 2. Check average value jq '.aggregations.agent_stats.avg' /tmp/agent_stats.json # 3. Check standard deviation jq '.aggregations.agent_stats.std_deviation' /tmp/agent_stats.json # 4. Identify top anomalous agents (filter for 5x or more above average) AVG=$(jq '.aggregations.agent_stats.avg' /tmp/agent_stats.json) THRESHOLD=$(echo "$AVG * 5" | bc) jq --argjson th $THRESHOLD \ '.aggregations.by_agent.buckets | map(select(.doc_count >= $th)) | sort_by(-.doc_count) | .[0:10]' \ /tmp/agent_stats.json

Purpose: Identify the processes generating the most logs by ranking 9.6.1 OpenSearch DSL Query​ { "size": 0, "query": { "range": { "server_dt": { "gte": "2026-01-01", "lt": "2026-02-01" } } }, "aggs": { "by_process": { "terms": { "field": "process_nm.keyword", "size": 20, "order": { "_count": "desc" } } } } } 9.6.2 Manual Execution (OpenSearch Dashboards)​ You can create the following visualization in OpenSearch Dashboards: Visualize → Lens or Vertical Bar Chart Index Pattern: 00133fkfkg_* Metrics: Count Buckets: Terms, process_nm.keyword, Top 20

Purpose: Analyze growth rates of MariaDB log tables and automatically recommend items requiring in-depth analysis 9.7.1 MariaDB Query Structure​ Current month and previous month count query: -- January 2026 count SELECT COUNT(*) FROM TPU_ISTAT_MEDIA_EVENT_LOG WHERE server_dt >= '2026-01-01' AND server_dt < '2026-02-01'; -- December 2025 count (previous month) SELECT COUNT(*) FROM TPU_ISTAT_MEDIA_EVENT_LOG WHERE server_dt >= '2025-12-01' AND server_dt < '2026-01-01'; 9.7.2 Analysis Target Tables​ Table Name Analysis Type Recommended Command TPU_ISTAT_MEDIA_EVENT_LOG Media control logs tkctl analyze media-top TPU_ISTAT_EVENT_LOG Event logs tkctl analyze process-top TPU_ISTAT_SYS_LOG System logs tkctl analyze process-top 9.7.3 Growth Rate Calculation Formula​ Growth Rate (%) = (Current Month Count - Previous Month Count) / Previous Month Count x 100 9.7.4 Priority Assessment Criteria​ Priority Condition Display Icon Recommended Action 1 (Surge) Growth rate >= 100% Red indicator Immediate analysis required 2 (Rapid increase) 50% <= Growth rate < 100% Warning indicator Root cause analysis recommended 3 (Normal) Growth rate < 50% OK indicator Within normal range 9.7.5 Manual Analysis Script​ #!/bin/bash # Manual execution of recommend analysis DBNAME="00133FKFKG" MONTH="2026-01" PREV_MONTH="2025-12" # Media control log growth rate analysis CURRENT=$(mysql -u tachyon -p'password' $DBNAME -N -e \ "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM TPU_ISTAT_MEDIA_EVENT_LOG WHERE server_dt >= '${MONTH}-01' AND server_dt < DATE_ADD('${MONTH}-01', INTERVAL 1 MONTH);") PREVIOUS=$(mysql -u tachyon -p'password' $DBNAME -N -e \ "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM TPU_ISTAT_MEDIA_EVENT_LOG WHERE server_dt >= '${PREV_MONTH}-01' AND server_dt < '${MONTH}-01';") if [ "$PREVIOUS" -gt 0 ]; then RATE=$(echo "scale=1; ($CURRENT - $PREVIOUS) * 100 / $PREVIOUS" | bc) echo "Media control logs: Current=${CURRENT}, Previous=${PREVIOUS}, Growth rate=${RATE}%" if (( $(echo "$RATE >= 100" | bc -l) )); then echo "CRITICAL - Immediate analysis required: tkctl analyze media-top --month $MONTH" elif (( $(echo "$RATE >= 50" | bc -l) )); then echo "WARNING - Root cause analysis recommended: tkctl analyze media-top --month $MONTH" else echo "OK - Within normal range" fi fi

Purpose: Track cumulative growth trends of LOG tables by period (1 week, 1 month, 1 quarter, 1 year) 9.8.1 MariaDB Query Structure​ Cumulative count query by period: -- Cumulative count up to 1 week ago (Start Total) SELECT COUNT(*) FROM TPU_ISTAT_MEDIA_EVENT_LOG WHERE server_dt < DATE_SUB(CURDATE(), INTERVAL 7 DAY); -- Cumulative count up to current (End Total) SELECT COUNT(*) FROM TPU_ISTAT_MEDIA_EVENT_LOG WHERE server_dt < DATE_ADD(CURDATE(), INTERVAL 1 DAY); 9.8.2 Growth Rate Calculation​ Period Increase Count = End Total - Start Total Growth Rate (%) = (End Total - Start Total) / Start Total x 100 9.8.3 Trend Assessment Criteria​ Trend Growth Rate Condition Display Surge (Critical) >= 50% Red indicator Rapid increase (Warning) 20% ~ 50% Orange indicator Gradual (Normal) 5% ~ 20% Upward trend Stable 0% ~ 5% Flat Decrease < 0% Downward trend 9.8.4 Manual Analysis Query Example​ -- Growth trend for the past week SET @start_total = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM TPU_ISTAT_MEDIA_EVENT_LOG WHERE server_dt < DATE_SUB(CURDATE(), INTERVAL 7 DAY)); SET @end_total = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM TPU_ISTAT_MEDIA_EVENT_LOG); SET @increase = @end_total - @start_total; SET @rate = IF(@start_total > 0, (@increase / @start_total) * 100, 100); SELECT 'Past 1 week' AS period, @start_total AS start_cnt, @end_total AS end_cnt, @increase AS increase, CONCAT(ROUND(@rate, 1), '%') AS growth_rate, CASE WHEN @rate >= 50 THEN 'CRITICAL - Surge' WHEN @rate >= 20 THEN 'WARNING - Rapid increase' WHEN @rate >= 5 THEN 'Gradual (Normal)' WHEN @rate > 0 THEN 'Stable' ELSE 'Decrease' END AS trend;
documents.s: 9.8 db-trend: DB Data Growth Trend Analysis

documents.t: How to verify the OpenSearch connection information used by tkctl analyze: 9.9.1 Configuration File Location​ # Default configuration file (may be encrypted) cat /usr/local/TACHYON/TTS40/conf/app_info.properties # Plaintext original (varies by operational environment) cat /usr/local/TACHYON/TTS40/conf/app_info.properties_dev* 9.9.2 Key Configuration Items​ opensearch_ip=127.0.0.1 opensearch_port=9200 opensearch_id=admin opensearch_pw=admin 9.9.3 tkctl.ini Individual Configuration (Higher Priority)​ You can specify the OpenSearch URL separately in the tkctl.ini file: [opensearch] url=http://localhost:9200

When using the --output option, results are saved in the following formats: 9.10.1 CSV Format (.csv)​ RANK,PROCESS,PATH,COUNT 1,C:\Windows\Explorer.EXE,,42349 2,C:\Windows\system32\svchost.exe,,5966 3,C:\Program Files\Windows Defender\MsMpEng.exe,E:\,2611 Encoding: UTF-8 with BOM (Excel compatible) Delimiter: Comma (,) Line ending: CRLF 9.10.2 TXT Format (.txt)​ Saved as a text file in table format.

9.11.1 OpenSearch Connection Failure​ OpenSearch call failed: dial tcp 127.0.0.1:9200: connect: connection refused Causes and remediation: Check OpenSearch service: systemctl status opensearch Check port listening: ss -tlnp | grep 9200 Check firewall: firewall-cmd --list-ports 9.11.2 Index Not Found Error​ No data available for the specified period. Causes and remediation: Verify index exists: curl "http://localhost:9200/_cat/indices?v" | grep {spcode} Check date range: Verify that data exists for the specified month Check SPCODE: Confirm the organization code in the index name is correct 9.11.3 MariaDB Connection Failure​ DB connection failed: dial tcp 127.0.0.1:3306: connect: connection refused Causes and remediation: Check MariaDB service: systemctl status mariadb Verify connection info: db_ip, db_port, db_user, db_password in app_info.properties

9.12.1 Detailed Analysis of a Specific Process​ # Detailed analysis of access paths for Explorer.EXE curl -X POST "http://localhost:9200/00133fkfkg_2026_01/_search" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{ "size": 0, "query": { "bool": { "must": [ { "range": { "server_dt": { "gte": "2026-01-01", "lt": "2026-02-01" } } }, { "match": { "process_nm": "Explorer.EXE" } } ] } }, "aggs": { "by_path": { "terms": { "field": "file_nm.keyword", "size": 50 } } } }' | jq '.aggregations.by_path.buckets' 9.12.2 Detailed Event Query for a Specific Agent​ # Query the 100 most recent events for an anomalous agent curl -X POST "http://localhost:9200/00133fkfkg_2026_01/_search" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{ "size": 100, "query": { "bool": { "must": [ { "term": { "puid.keyword": "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890" } } ] } }, "sort": [{ "server_dt": "desc" }], "_source": ["server_dt", "process_nm", "file_nm", "event_type"] }' | jq '.hits.hits[]._source' 9.12.3 Daily Log Volume Trend​ # Daily log count aggregation curl -X POST "http://localhost:9200/00133fkfkg_2026_01/_search" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{ "size": 0, "aggs": { "by_day": { "terms": { "field": "server_dt", "size": 31, "order": { "_key": "asc" } } } } }' | jq '.aggregations.by_day.buckets' Note: The queries in this document are based on the default schema of the TACHYON 4.0 system. Field names and index structures may differ in actual operational environments, so please verify the schema before use.
Backs up and restores MariaDB and OpenSearch data. Implemented with Cobra native subcommand structure, providing seamless auto-completion and help support.
# Backup tkctl backup [mariadb|opensearch] [flags] # Restore tkctl restore [mariadb|opensearch] [args]

Uses mariabackup to back up the MariaDB database. Usage: # Full backup (default) tkctl backup mariadb # Specify backup type tkctl backup mariadb --type full # Full physical backup tkctl backup mariadb --type incr # Incremental backup tkctl backup mariadb --type config # Configuration data only (excludes logs) tkctl backup mariadb --type log # Full backup including logs # Combined options tkctl backup mariadb --type full --retention 30 --compress Flags: Flag Short Description Default --type -t Backup type (config, log, full, incr) full --retention -r Retention days (type-aware default — Log 90, Config 30, Full/Incr 30. Override when explicitly specified) type-aware --compress -c Use qpress compression (type-aware default — Log forced true, Config false, Full/Incr true. Override when explicitly specified) type-aware --incremental-basedir Base directory for incremental backup (auto-detected) Output paths (per-type, BL-001 v0.6.42+): --type Output directory Compression (.qp) Prepare Default retention full ${BACKUP_DIR}/full_$DATE/ optional ✅ (ready for restore) 30 days incr ${BACKUP_DIR}/incr_$DATE/ optional ❌ 30 days log ${BACKUP_DIR}/weekly_log/$DATE/ forced (qpress required) ❌ 90 days config ${BACKUP_DIR}/daily_config/$DATE/ default false ❌ 30 days Output example (--type log): 📦 MariaDB Backup started (6 steps) [ 16%] [0s] Preflight: checking backup environment... [ 33%] [0s] Preparing: building mariabackup command... [ 50%] [0s] Running: mariabackup --backup in progress... [ 66%] [43s] Prepare: verifying backup consistency... [ 83%] [43s] Metadata: saving backup info... [100%] [43s] Cleanup: pruning old backups... ✅ MariaDB Backup (log) completed successfully. Path: /usr/local/TACHYON/TTS40/mariadb/backup/weekly_log/20260427_233227 Size: 3.56 GB Duration: 43s 💡 Progress bar: Since v0.4.23, a visual progress bar is displayed in TTY environments. When output is piped (|) or redirected (>), the traditional text format is shown. ⚠️ --type log qpress requirement (v0.6.42+) The Log type forces compression. If qpress is not found in the system PATH or in ${BaseDir}/mariadb/scripts/backup/bin/, the command fails immediately (typed error [1009] Log backup requires qpress compression but qpress binary was not found, exit code 1). Operator actions: Install via system package: dnf install qpress or apt install qpress Or deploy to local path: ${BaseDir}/mariadb/scripts/backup/bin/qpress (must be chmod +x) Configuration File Priority Principle Authentication uses ${BaseDir}/conf/app_info.properties_dev's db_user / db_password as the single source of truth (SSOT). Environment variables MYSQL_USER / MYSQL_PASS / MYSQL_PORT are ignored (LogWarn) when they differ from the configuration file. tkcli's backup.env is not supported. Multi-process file lock (v0.6.42+, BL-006) When cron-driven backups and manual CLI invocations target the same BackupDir simultaneously, the second caller fails immediately with [1004] another backup is in progress (PID=N). Lockfiles are split per type — ${BackupDir}/.tkctl-mariadb-{full|incr|log|config}.lock — so different types may still run concurrently. Aspect Linux/macOS Windows Mechanism syscall.Flock(LOCK_EX|LOCK_NB) kernel advisory lock O_EXCL lockfile fallback (advisory only) Auto-release ✅ Released on fd close at process exit — no stale residue ⚠️ Lockfile may persist after abnormal termination Stale recovery Not required If the recorded PID is dead, the operator must remove it manually: rm ${BackupDir}/.tkctl-mariadb-*.lock Diagnostic branches: a contention (other holder) returns [1004 BACKUP_IN_PROGRESS] + holder PID. A write-permission shortage on BackupDir (e.g. an unprivileged user invoking against a root-owned directory) returns [1005 INVALID_BACKUP_DIR] + operator guidance (BackupDir write permission denied — run as root or with sufficient privileges), so first-line triage is unambiguous. Operational guidance: distribute cron schedules to avoid temporal overlap with manual CLI runs. Even when they collide, the second call is blocked instead of advancing into database lock contention or backup corruption. In production, run tkctl backup mariadb with root privileges. --dry-run Preflight (v0.6.42+, BL-007)​ Run a 6-item preflight check without performing an actual backup. Useful for validating cron registration ahead of time or for post-incident triage. $ tkctl backup mariadb --dry-run --type log PREFLIGHT type=log [OK] mariabackup-binary /usr/local/TACHYON/TTS40/mariadb/bin/mariabackup (MariaDB 10.11.14-MariaDB) [OK] qpress-binary /usr/local/TACHYON/TTS40/mariadb/scripts/backup/bin/qpress [OK] backup-dir-write /usr/local/TACHYON/TTS40/mariadb/backup [OK] data-dir-access /usr/local/TACHYON/TTS40/mariadb/data (123 entries) [OK] lockfile-available .tkctl-mariadb-log.lock [OK] credentials user=TACHYON port=3306 password=set (9 chars) source=app_info PASS — backup ready Check What it validates type=log type=config type=full/incr mariabackup-binary Binary discovered + exec bit + --version parsed required required required qpress-binary Binary discovered + exec bit required optional (SKIP) optional (SKIP) backup-dir-write probe file create/remove required required required data-dir-access Exists + is directory + readable required required required lockfile-available No other holder (acquire+release) required required required credentials user present + password length/source shown (plaintext never echoed) required required required Exit code Meaning 0 All checks OK or SKIP — backup can proceed (PASS line) 1 One or more FAIL — operator action needed (FAIL line + per-check detail) Minimal side effects --dry-run only creates a transient .preflight-probe-<PID> file (immediately removed) in BackupDir and acquires/releases the lockfile once. If another backup of the same type is in progress, the run reports [FAIL] lockfile-available immediately and recommends a retry. --with-connect — DB credential accuracy ping (BL-007 follow-up)​ The default --dry-run only verifies that credentials are loaded (the credentials check). A wrong password still reports as "set (N chars)" / OK. Pass --with-connect to add a SELECT 1 ping against the live mariadb server and verify accuracy as well. $ tkctl backup mariadb --dry-run --with-connect --type log PREFLIGHT type=log [OK ] mariabackup-binary /usr/local/.../mariadb-backup (MariaDB 10.11.14-MariaDB) [OK ] qpress-binary /usr/local/bin/qpress [OK ] backup-dir-write /usr/local/TACHYON/TTS40/mariadb/backup [OK ] data-dir-access /usr/local/TACHYON/TTS40/mariadb/data (18 entries) [OK ] lockfile-available .tkctl-mariadb-log.lock [OK ] credentials user=root port=3306 password=set (9 chars) source=app_info [OK ] db-connect-ping 127.0.0.1:3306 (SELECT 1 OK, timeout=3s) PASS — backup ready Flag Default Description --with-connect false Adds db-connect-ping after the standard 6 checks --connect-timeout 3s Ping timeout (Go time.Duration, e.g. 5s, 1m) --connect-host "" (→ 127.0.0.1) TCP host override for non-localhost mariadb. Mutually exclusive with --connect-socket --connect-socket "" (→ TCP) Unix domain socket path (e.g. /var/run/mysqld/mysqld.sock). When set, host is ignored Non-localhost / Unix socket examples: # Remote mariadb (e.g. read replica) $ tkctl backup mariadb --dry-run --with-connect --connect-host 192.168.1.100 --type log # Unix socket (performance-tuned environments) $ tkctl backup mariadb --dry-run --with-connect --connect-socket /var/run/mysqld/mysqld.sock --type log host / socket are mutually exclusive Specifying both --connect-host and --connect-socket is rejected immediately (mutually exclusive). When socket is set, host is ignored and the diagnostic target is reported as unix:<path>. If the socket file is missing, the run reports [FAIL] db-connect-ping ... Unix socket 미존재 before invoking the driver, so first-line triage is unambiguous. Scenario Result No credentials [SKIP] db-connect-ping (the credentials check already explains why) Wrong password [FAIL] db-connect-ping + Access denied (with password redacted) DB down / port closed [FAIL] db-connect-ping + connection refused or timeout Valid credentials + live DB [OK] db-connect-ping Host assumption The DSN is hard-coded to tcp(127.0.0.1:<port>), assuming tkctl runs locally on the database server. Non-localhost mariadb or Unix-socket setups are out of scope (separate follow-up). --with-connect only takes effect under --dry-run; in a real backup, mariabackup itself enforces credential validity.

Creates an index snapshot in the OpenSearch snapshot repository. Usage: # Initialize snapshot repository (one-time setup) tkctl backup opensearch --init-repo # Create snapshot (auto-named) tkctl backup opensearch # Create snapshot with specified name tkctl backup opensearch snap_20260116 Flags: Flag Description --init-repo Initialize snapshot repository (fs type). ⚠️ If path.repo is missing in opensearch.yml, the OpenSearch service is automatically restarted (~30s downtime) --username OpenSearch username (default: auto-detect) --password OpenSearch password (default: auto-detect) ⚠️ --init-repo Auto-Restart Policy (v0.6.39) When --init-repo runs and path.repo is missing from opensearch.yml, the following steps are performed in a single command: Auto-append path.repo to opensearch.yml Display warning banner + 3-second grace period (Ctrl+C to abort) Auto-execute systemctl restart opensearch Poll /_cluster/health and wait up to 90 seconds for cluster readiness Register the snapshot repository OpenSearch connectivity is briefly interrupted (~30s) during the restart. Run during a maintenance window or coordinate with the operations team. If path.repo is already configured, no restart occurs. Output example (path.repo missing → auto-restart case): 2026-04-13 19:10:02 [INFO] Initializing OpenSearch Snapshot Repository 'backup'... 2026-04-13 19:10:02 [WARN] path.repo not configured in opensearch.yml — auto-appending. 2026-04-13 19:10:02 [SUCC] path.repo appended to opensearch.yml. 2026-04-13 19:10:02 [WARN] ======================================================================== 2026-04-13 19:10:02 [WARN] ⚠️ Auto-restarting OpenSearch service (required for path.repo) 2026-04-13 19:10:02 [WARN] → OpenSearch connectivity will be interrupted briefly (~30s) 2026-04-13 19:10:02 [WARN] → Press Ctrl+C within 3s to abort 2026-04-13 19:10:02 [WARN] ======================================================================== 2026-04-13 19:10:05 [INFO] Executing: systemctl restart opensearch 2026-04-13 19:10:08 [SUCC] OpenSearch restart issued — waiting for cluster readiness... 2026-04-13 19:10:25 [SUCC] OpenSearch is ready — proceeding with repository registration. 2026-04-13 19:10:25 [SUCC] Repository initialized successfully. Output example (normal snapshot creation): 2026-01-16 10:35:00 [INFO] Creating OpenSearch snapshot... 2026-01-16 10:35:00 [INFO] Snapshot name: snap_20260116_103500 2026-01-16 10:35:10 [SUCC] Snapshot created successfully.

Restores the MariaDB database from a mariabackup backup. Usage: # Restore from full backup tkctl restore mariadb /backup/full_20260116 # Restore from full + incremental backups tkctl restore mariadb /backup/full_20260116 /backup/incr_20260117 /backup/incr_20260118 Output example: 2026-01-16 11:00:00 [INFO] Starting MariaDB restore... 2026-01-16 11:00:00 [INFO] Full backup: /backup/full_20260116 2026-01-16 11:00:00 [WARN] MariaDB service is running. Please stop it before restoring. 2026-01-16 11:00:00 [INFO] Command: systemctl stop mariadb Caution: The MariaDB service must be stopped before restoring.

Restores OpenSearch indices from a saved snapshot. Usage: tkctl restore opensearch snap_20260116 Output example: 2026-01-16 11:10:00 [INFO] Starting OpenSearch snapshot restore... 2026-01-16 11:10:00 [INFO] Snapshot: snap_20260116 2026-01-16 11:10:15 [SUCC] Snapshot restore completed.
This section covers the basic commands (version, info, admin, completion, update) for checking and maintaining the overall system status.

Command Function Key Flags (Options) (default) View detailed system and component versions None Checks version information of TKCTL and related middleware. Provides detailed version information of all TACHYON components and middleware in table format, and detailed descriptions can be viewed via --help.

Command Function Key Flags (Options) (default) View system resources and disk usage None Provides an overview of license information, system resources, disk usage, and service status. Detailed information is automatically collected and displayed from the /usr/local/TACHYON path and related middleware data.

2025-12-24 21:02:27 [INFO] ### TACHYON System Information ### --- License and Organization Info --- ------------------------------------------------------ Asset Item Value ------------------------------------------------------ Organization Name TSGroup - Offline Organization Code 00133FKFKG Agent Count 100 ------------------------------------------------------ --- System Resources --- --------------------------------------- Resource Usage --------------------------------------- Total Physical Memory 15 GB Free Memory 0 GB --------------------------------------- --- Disk Usage --- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ Category Size Path ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ Installation Directory 7.7G /usr/local/TACHYON Database 2.1G /usr/local/TACHYON/TTS40/mariadb/data OpenSearch 15G /usr/local/TACHYON/TTS40/opensearch/opensearch/data ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ 2025-12-24 21:02:28 [SUCC] Service status check completed. Note: OS Memory Over-Utilization Information The memory usage reported by tkctl info includes OS-level physical memory utilization. Especially in Linux environments, idle memory is used as Page Cache/Buffer for performance optimization, so the displayed usage may appear higher than the actual process usage — this is known as "over-utilization." This is not a system failure but a normal result of OS performance management. For detailed technical explanation, refer to [Appendix A - 8. OS Memory Management Specification].

The admin command is a subcommand system for managing TACHYON administrator accounts. Subcommand Function Arguments reset [user_id] Reset administrator password Target user ID reset-ip [user_id] Reset administrator IP ACL Target user ID superuser -P <password> Change tkadmin superuser password -P : New password reset — Password Reset​ Resets the password of an administrator account. Usage​ tkctl admin reset <USER_ID> Reset Password​ !TumsAdmin2025 Examples​ # Reset admin account password tkctl admin reset admin # Reset specific user password tkctl admin reset user123 Output Example​ 2025-12-07 16:15:00[INFO] Updating password for user ID 'admin'... 2025-12-07 16:15:01[SUCC] Password update for user 'admin' completed. 2025-12-07 16:15:01[INFO] New password and key have been set. reset-ip — IP ACL Reset​ Clears the IP access control (ACL) settings for a TACHYON administrator account. Use this command when an account is locked out due to IP ACL restrictions. Usage​ tkctl admin reset-ip <USER_ID> Examples​ # Clear IP ACL for tsadmin account tkctl admin reset-ip tsadmin Output Example​ 2026-03-30 10:00:00[INFO] Resetting IP ACL for user 'tsadmin'... 2026-03-30 10:00:00[INFO] Current IP ACL: allow_ip1=192.168.1.10, allow_ip2=(none) 2026-03-30 10:00:01[SUCC] IP ACL for user 'tsadmin' has been reset. info Changes are applied to both 000TACHYON.TPU_WCONSOLE_USERS (global) and the tenant table.
Command Function Key Flags (Options) (default) Generate shell auto-completion scripts [bash/zsh/fish/powershell] Generates auto-completion scripts for various shells.

Bash (Linux): # System-wide tkctl completion bash | sudo tee /etc/bash_completion.d/tkctl # Per-user tkctl completion bash > ~/.local/share/bash-completion/completions/tkctl Zsh: # Enable completion (first time only) echo "autoload -U compinit; compinit" >> ~/.zshrc # Install completion script tkctl completion zsh > "${fpath[1]}/_tkctl" Fish: tkctl completion fish > ~/.config/fish/completions/tkctl.fish

Starting from v0.4.20, all file path completions are disabled, and intelligent auto-completion features have been significantly enhanced. Static Completion: Suggests predefined values immediately for applied flags. Flag Applied Commands Completion Values --format analyze, backup json, csv, table --level Global debug, info, warn, error --target service, fix all, tachyon, middleware --shell completion bash, zsh, fish --status analyze security vulnerable, good, manual --source analyze opensearch, mariadb, filesystem Dynamic Completion: Queries real-time index/field lists through OpenSearch connections to provide suggestions. Flag Applied Commands Behavior --index backup opensearch, analyze Real-time index list query --field analyze media-top, etc. Field list query for selected index --pattern backup opensearch ilm Index pattern suggestion --policy backup opensearch ilm ILM policy list query --alias backup opensearch Alias list query Usage Examples: # Static completion tkctl analyze --format <Tab> # json csv table # Dynamic completion (requires OpenSearch connection) tkctl backup opensearch index --index <Tab> # tachyon-agent-2026.01 tachyon-media-2026.01 ...

Command Function Key Flags (Options) nginx Nginx update -f : Package file path (required) --force : Skip version check and force proceed redis Redis update -f : Package file path (required) opensearch OpenSearch update -f : Package file path (required) --force : Skip version check and force proceed Updates the key middleware services (Nginx, Redis, OpenSearch) of the TACHYON system. Rather than simple file replacement, a version verification system is built in to prevent system failures caused by incorrect version installations.
--file, -f: Specifies the path to the update package file (.tar.gz). --force: Ignores version safety checks and forces the update to proceed. (Useful in downgrade scenarios)

Downgrade Prevention: Automatically stops the update if the package version is lower than the currently installed version. Major Version Protection: Outputs a warning when the first digit (Major) of the version differs (e.g., v2 -> v3) to raise awareness of compatibility issues in advance. Integrity Check: After decompression, directly executes the binary to extract and verify version information.

$ tkctl update nginx -f nginx-1.29.4.tar.gz 2024-12-21 15:30:10 [INFO] Starting nginx update... 2024-12-21 15:30:10 [INFO] Version verification: Current 1.29.3 -> Target 1.29.4 (safe) ... 2024-12-21 15:30:15 [SUCC] nginx update completed.

Generates middleware packages for deployment to production environments (air-gapped networks, etc.). Downloads the latest source from an internet-connected environment, compiles binaries optimized for the production server specifications, and repackages them.

nginx: Downloads latest stable source and includes OpenSSL/PCRE2/Zlib static compilation. redis: Downloads and compiles the latest source. opensearch: Repackages based on official binary. mariadb: Downloads binary tarball via MariaDB REST API (LTS 10.11 default). Supports archive.mariadb.org fallback when REST API fails.

Automatically checks for required build tools on the system before packaging. Checks: gcc, make, perl (including FindBin module), tar, curl Auto-Install: If running with root privileges, automatically installs missing tools via dnf or apt. Manual Install Guide: If privileges are insufficient or auto-install is not possible, displays optimized installation commands for the system. $ tkctl update package nginx 2025-12-23 11:00:05 [INFO] Checking build dependencies... 2025-12-23 11:00:05 [WARN] Missing dependencies found: gcc, perl 2025-12-23 11:00:05 [INFO] Automatically installing required packages... ... 2025-12-23 11:02:40 [SUCC] Build dependency installation complete. Continuing with packaging. Normal Upgrade Scenario $ tkctl update opensearch --file opensearch-2.19.4.tar.gz Output: 2025-12-20 12:16:34[INFO] Target service: opensearch, File: /root/packages/opensearch-2.19.4.tar.gz 2025-12-20 12:16:34[INFO] Starting OpenSearch update (source: /root/packages/opensearch-2.19.4.tar.gz) 2025-12-20 12:16:34[INFO] Stopping opensearch service... 2025-12-20 12:16:34[INFO] Extracting archive... 2025-12-20 12:16:49[INFO] Version check: [Current: 2.18.0] -> [New: 2.19.4] 2025-12-20 12:16:49[SUCC] Version upgrade confirmed: 2.18.0 -> 2.19.4 2025-12-20 12:16:49[INFO] Syncing files (rsync)... 2025-12-20 12:16:53[INFO] Starting opensearch service... 2025-12-20 12:16:54[SUCC] OpenSearch update completed successfully. # Force recovery (Downgrade) scenario tkctl update opensearch --file opensearch-2.19.4.tar.gz --force # Output: 2025-12-20 12:24:49[INFO] Version check: [Current: 3.4.0] -> [New: 2.19.4] 2025-12-20 12:24:49[WARN] Critical: Major version mismatch! Compatibility issues may occur. 2025-12-20 12:24:49[WARN] Skipping safety check due to --force flag. Proceeding with older version (2.19.4 < 3.4.0). 2025-12-20 12:24:49[INFO] Syncing files (rsync)... 2025-12-20 12:24:54[SUCC] OpenSearch update completed successfully.

Command Function Key Flags (Options) nginx Generate Nginx package --version, --clean-cache redis Generate Redis package --version, --clean-cache opensearch Download OpenSearch package --version, --clean-cache mariadb Download MariaDB package --version, --clean-cache

Flag Short Description --version -V Specify a particular version (e.g., --version 10.11.11) --clean-cache - Clean cache directory before build Provides expert functionality for professional engineers to download, compile, and package the latest middleware versions for air-gapped environment deployment. This feature tracks the currently installed major version to maintain compatibility with the production environment.

Major Version Protection: If currently using v1.x, even if v2.x is released, it automatically finds and packages the latest patch version in the v1.x series. Automatic Dependency Resolution: Automatically downloads libraries such as PCRE2, OpenSSL, and Zlib needed for Nginx builds along with the source for static compilation. Standardized Filenames: Generates packages with version-explicit filenames like nginx-1.29.4.tar.gz.

# Generate Nginx latest patch package tkctl update package nginx # Output: 2025-12-20 12:12:40[INFO] Checking internet connection... 2025-12-20 12:12:41[SUCC] Internet connection confirmed. 2025-12-20 12:12:41[INFO] Currently installed version: 1.29.3 (major: 1) 2025-12-20 12:12:41[INFO] Detecting latest stable version for nginx (filtering major 1)... 2025-12-20 12:12:42[INFO] Target version (1.29.4) is newer than current (1.29.3). Package update is recommended. 2025-12-20 12:12:43[INFO] - Downloading dependency: pcre2-10.45 2025-12-20 12:12:48[INFO] - Configuring: --with-pcre=../pcre2-10.45 ... 2025-12-20 12:12:53[INFO] - Compiling... 2025-12-20 12:14:25[SUCC] Package generated successfully: /root/dist/packages/nginx-1.29.4.tar.gz # Generate MariaDB latest LTS package (auto-detect when version not specified) tkctl update package mariadb # Output: 2026-02-09 16:59:51[INFO] Checking internet connection... 2026-02-09 16:59:52[SUCC] Internet connection confirmed. 2026-02-09 16:59:52[INFO] Currently installed version: 10.11.2 (major: 10.11) 2026-02-09 16:59:52[INFO] Detecting latest stable version for mariadb (filtering major 10.11)... 2026-02-09 16:59:52[INFO] Detecting latest version in MariaDB 10.11 series... 2026-02-09 16:59:54[INFO] Target version (10.11.16) is newer than current (10.11.2). Package update is recommended. 2026-02-09 16:59:54[INFO] Downloading mariadb 10.11.16 source/artifacts... 2026-02-09 17:01:12[INFO] Building and packaging mariadb 10.11.16... 2026-02-09 17:01:20[SUCC] Package generated successfully: /root/dist/packages/mariadb-10.11.16.tar.gz Automatic Version Detection (v0.5.7): When no version is specified, it automatically extracts the major series of the currently installed MariaDB (e.g., 10.11), and detects the latest patch version via REST API (downloads.mariadb.org) or archive (archive.mariadb.org) fallback. # Specify a particular version (--version flag) tkctl update package mariadb --version 10.11.10 tkctl update package nginx -V 1.24.0 # Generate packages for all services tkctl update package all

This command must be executed in an environment with active internet connection (e.g., development server, internet-accessible production server). Generated packages can be copied to air-gapped servers and installed using the tkctl update command.

Nginx and Redis package generation requires source code compilation, so the following build tools must be installed on the system: Component Description RHEL/Rocky/Alma Package Ubuntu/Debian Package GCC C/C++ compiler gcc, gcc-c++ build-essential Make Build automation tool make make Perl Required for OpenSSL build perl-core, perl-IPC-Cmd perl Tar Archive handling tar tar Note: OpenSearch and MariaDB download pre-built binaries, so compilation tools are not required.

tkctl automatically checks build dependencies before packaging and attempts automatic installation if missing packages are detected. How It Works: Auto-detects the OS distribution (RHEL, Rocky, Ubuntu, etc.). Identifies the package manager (dnf, yum, apt). When running as root, automatically installs missing packages. If auto-installation is not possible, provides manual installation commands. Output Example (Auto Dependency Installation): 2025-12-23 17:00:00[INFO] Checking build dependencies... 2025-12-23 17:00:00[WARN] Missing build dependencies: gcc, perl 2025-12-23 17:00:00[INFO] Automatically installing missing packages... 2025-12-23 17:00:00[INFO] Installing packages via dnf: gcc, gcc-c++, perl-core, perl-IPC-Cmd ... 2025-12-23 17:00:15[SUCC] Build dependency installation completed. Manual Installation (When Auto-Install Fails): # RHEL 9 / Rocky Linux 9 / AlmaLinux 9 sudo dnf install -y gcc gcc-c++ make perl-core perl-IPC-Cmd # Ubuntu / Debian sudo apt-get install -y build-essential make perl

Command Function Key Flags (Options) (default) View all environment configuration status --set : Automatically apply recommended values for each item selinux SELinux configuration [mode] : enforcing/permissive/disabled --set : Apply configuration ulimit System limits configuration [value] : Open Files limit value (e.g., 65535) --set : Apply configuration firewall Firewall port configuration [port] : Port to open --set : Apply configuration (includes defaults like SSH, 443) ssh SSH port configuration [port] : Port number to change to --set : Restart service and sync with firewall web Web port configuration [port] : Port number to change to --set : Restart service and sync with firewall db DB port configuration [port] : Port number to change to --set : Apply configuration --yes : Auto-approve service restart locale System Locale configuration [lang] : Language code (ko/en) --set : Apply configuration service-limit Service LimitNOFILE configuration [service] [value] : Service name + file descriptor limit Alias: svc-limit Diagnoses and configures the OS environment (SELinux, Ulimit, Firewall, SSH) optimized for running the Tachyon solution. Use the tkctl env [subcommand] format for detailed control of each item. Running tkctl env without arguments performs a summary check of all items. The --set (or -s) option is used to actually apply configurations. When arguments are omitted, the TACHYON recommended values appropriate for each environment are automatically selected.

Checks and optimizes the current SELinux runtime mode and permanent configuration. Usage: # Check status tkctl env selinux # Automatically set to recommended value (disabled) tkctl env selinux --set # Set to a specific mode tkctl env selinux disabled --set tkctl env selinux permissive --set Features: Skips redundant operations if already set to the target mode. When setting disabled mode, changes the permanent configuration and applies permissive at runtime for immediate effect until reboot.

Diagnoses the system-wide (/etc/security/limits.conf) and per-service LimitNOFILE configuration status. Usage: # Check limits status tkctl env ulimit # Automatically set system limit to recommended value (65535) tkctl env ulimit --set # Set system limit to a specific value tkctl env ulimit 99999 --set Note: Applying system limit settings requires logging out and back in or a system restart.

Checks the system firewall (firewalld) status and opens ports required for TACHYON operation. Usage: # Diagnose firewall status and open ports tkctl env firewall # Automatically open TACHYON default ports (SSH, 443) tkctl env firewall --set # Open a specific additional port tkctl env firewall 8080 --set Configuration logic: No arguments: Automatically detects the current system's SSH port and opens it along with port 443. Port specified: Opens the specified port with the tcp protocol.

2025-12-24 21:03:35 [INFO] ### Firewall Configuration Diagnostics ### [ Firewall Status ] -------------------------------------------- Service/Zone Status -------------------------------------------- firewalld Inactive iptables Inactive -------------------------------------------- 2025-12-24 21:03:35 [SUCC] Environment check complete.

Checks and changes the OpenSSH service port number. Usage: # Check current SSH port tkctl env ssh # Change SSH port to 2022 tkctl env ssh 2022 --set # Revert SSH port to default (22) tkctl env ssh --set Automatic configuration notes: Firewall integration: When changing the port via --set, the new port is automatically allowed in the firewall. (Prevents connection blocking) Automatic service restart: After configuration changes, the sshd service is automatically restarted for immediate effect.

2025-12-24 21:03:46 [INFO] ### SSH (OpenSSH) Configuration Diagnostics ### [ ### SSH (OpenSSH) Configuration Diagnostics ### ] -------------------------------------------- Item SSH Port -------------------------------------------- OpenSSH 22 -------------------------------------------- Use '--set <port>' to change the SSH port 2025-12-24 21:03:46 [SUCC] Environment check complete.

Checks and changes the listening port of the TACHYON web service (Nginx HTTPS). Usage: # Check current web port tkctl env web # Change HTTPS port to 8443 tkctl env web 8443 --set # Revert to default (443) tkctl env web --set v0.4.20+ Extended Options: Option Description --mode unified/separated Switch between unified/separated mode --console <port> Admin console port in separated mode (default: 8443) --backend <port> Backend API port in separated mode (default: 443) --dry-run Preview changes (no actual application) --restore Restore nginx.conf from backup Extended usage examples: # Switch to separated mode tkctl env web --mode separated --set # Separated mode + specify ports tkctl env web --mode separated --console 9443 --backend 10443 --set # Preview (check without changes) tkctl env web --mode separated --dry-run # Restore from backup tkctl env web --restore Detailed behavior: Nginx configuration change: Finds listen ... ssl directives in /etc/nginx/nginx.conf or conf.d/*.conf and changes the port. Firewall integration: Immediately allows the changed port in the firewall. Service control: Automatically restarts the Nginx service to apply changes. Automatic backup: Automatically backs up nginx.conf before configuration changes (.bak). Restore feature: Use the --restore option to restore settings from the last backup.

Changes the MariaDB database connection port and batch-synchronizes all TACHYON services and management tools that reference it. Usage: # Check current DB port tkctl env db # Change MariaDB port to 13306 (auto-approve) tkctl env db 13306 --set --yes # Revert to default (3306) tkctl env db 3306 --set Automation scope and impact: This command performs extensive system synchronization beyond a simple port change. System port change: Changes the MariaDB port in /etc/my.cnf.d/server.cnf. Batch service reference update: Searches all service configuration files (*.yml_dev) within TACHYON_HOME and replaces the port in jdbc:mariadb:// connection strings. Also updates the db_port entry in the global configuration (app_info.properties) to maintain system-wide consistency. Firewall and restart: Registers the changed port in the firewall. Sequentially restarts all affected services including MariaDB, API, Manager, Watchdog, and tkadmin. Since service downtime occurs during operation, user approval is requested unless the --yes option is provided. Caution (Nginx Stream): If Nginx uses a stream block (at the bottom of nginx.conf) to proxy the DB port (such as 3306), the port in this section is not automatically changed by default. v0.4.20+ Nginx Stream Auto-Sync: Option Description --sync-stream Automatically update nginx stream upstream port --dry-run Preview changes (no actual application) # Change DB port + auto-sync nginx stream tkctl env db 3307 --set --sync-stream # Preview (check without changes) tkctl env db 3307 --dry-run Using the --sync-stream option will also change the port in the upstream tachyon_mariadb block of nginx.conf. Output example: 2026-01-08 16:30:00 [INFO] Setting Database port to 13306... 2026-01-08 16:30:00 [INFO] Updating SERVICE configurations (TACHYON_HOME)... 2026-01-08 16:30:01 [INFO] Updated: api.yml_dev 2026-01-08 16:30:01 [INFO] Updated: manager.yml_dev ... 2026-01-08 16:30:02 [INFO] Updating nginx stream upstream 'tachyon_mariadb' to port 13306... 2026-01-08 16:30:02 [SUCC] Updated nginx stream upstream for MariaDB 2026-01-08 16:30:05 [INFO] Restarting TACHYON services... 2026-01-08 16:30:10 [SUCC] Database port updated to 13306

tkctl automatically detects the port when connecting to MariaDB in the following order: MariaDB configuration file: The port value specified in the [mysqld] section of my.cnf or server.cnf. Nginx Stream configuration: The Nginx upstream tachyon_mariadb port synchronized via tkctl env db --sync-stream. Default: 3306. Problem scenario: Changed DB port but tkctl cannot connect Symptom: dial tcp 127.0.0.1:3306: connect: connection refused error occurs. Cause: The port is not specified in the MariaDB configuration file and is not synchronized with the Nginx configuration, so the default (3306) is attempted. Solution: Method A (recommended): Run the tkctl env db <PORT> --sync-stream command to update the Nginx configuration, and tkctl will detect and connect to the correct port. Method B: Explicitly specify port=<PORT> in the [mysqld] section of the MariaDB configuration file (server.cnf, etc.). Tip: The tkctl env db command synchronizes the Nginx Stream configuration simultaneously with the port change, making it the safest way to change the port.
documents.s: 3.5.7 DB Port Detection and Connection Troubleshooting

documents.t: Item Recommended Value Command Example SELinux disabled tkctl env selinux --set Ulimit 65535 tkctl env ulimit --set Firewall ssh, 443, 3306 tkctl env firewall --set SSH Port 22 tkctl env ssh --set Web Port 443 tkctl env web --set DB Port 3306 tkctl env db --set Locale ko_KR.UTF-8 tkctl env locale ko --set Service Limit 65535 tkctl env service-limit nginx 65535

Checks and changes the system Locale (language and character set) configuration. Background: When installing RockyLinux 9 in Non-GUI (Minimal) mode, the default is set to LANG=C or LANG=en_US, which may cause Korean characters to display incorrectly in the product console. This can be resolved by changing to ko_KR.UTF-8 or en_US.UTF-8. Usage: # Check current locale status tkctl env locale # Set to Korean locale tkctl env locale ko --set # Set to English locale tkctl env locale en --set Features: If the current setting is already *.UTF-8, it skips without making changes. If the language pack (glibc-langpack-ko) is not installed, manual installation instructions are displayed. Air-gapped environment support: Outputs only a warning without automatic package installation. Output example: 2026-01-21 22:52:34 [INFO] ### Locale (Language/Character Set) Configuration ### ------------------------------------------------- Item Status ------------------------------------------------- System Locale LANG=ko_KR.UTF-8 VC Keymap kr Korean Language Pack ✓ Installed ------------------------------------------------- 2026-01-21 22:52:34 [SUCC] Environment check complete. When language pack is not installed: 2026-01-21 22:00:00 [WARN] Language pack (glibc-langpack-ko) is not installed. ℹ Install via ISO file or local repository: rpm -ivh glibc-langpack-ko-*.rpm

Checks and changes the systemd LimitNOFILE value for individual services. Usage: # View LimitNOFILE status for all services tkctl env service-limit # Change LimitNOFILE for a specific service tkctl env service-limit nginx 65535 tkctl env service-limit mariadb 131072 # Alias (svc-limit) also available tkctl env svc-limit redis 65535 Detailed behavior: Writes [Service]\nLimitNOFILE=<value> to /etc/systemd/system/<service>.service.d/override.conf Automatically performs systemctl daemon-reload + systemctl restart <service> Allowed range: 1024 ~ 1048576 Allowed services: mariadb, redis, zookeeper, kafka, opensearch, logstash-kafka-os, nginx, opensearch-dashboards, tkadmin Tab auto-completion: First argument: Service name auto-completion Second argument: Suggested values (4096, 8192, 16384, 32768, 65535, 131072)

Manages the Nginx web server's Access Control List (ACL) to restrict admin console access to specific IPs only. Supports IP addresses (IPv4) or CIDR ranges (e.g., 192.168.1.0/24). Scope of impact: ACL controls access to the admin console (frontend) only. It does not affect the backend API service (port 443), so API calls and service integrations are not affected. Usage: # 1. Check ACL policy tkctl env web acl list # 2. Add administrator IP (required) tkctl env web acl add 203.0.113.10 tkctl env web acl add 192.168.100.0/24 # 3. Enable ACL (blocks all connections, only allowed IPs can access) # Caution: Always add administrator IPs (add) before enabling (enable). tkctl env web acl enable # [Other] # Remove a specific allowed IP tkctl env web acl remove 203.0.113.10 # Disable ACL (allow all IP access) tkctl env web acl disable # Test configuration (syntax check and applied status verification) tkctl env web acl test Cautions: When ACL is enabled (enable), all connections are blocked (deny all) by default, and only IPs added with the add command are allowed. Localhost (127.0.0.1) is not automatically included in the allow rules for internal communication. Explicit addition is recommended depending on the location structure (explicit addition is recommended in the current implementation). After configuration changes (add, remove, enable, disable), Nginx must be restarted for changes to take effect. tkctl service restart nginx Configuration file behavior: By default, injects allow/deny directives into the http or location block of /app/nginx/conf/nginx.conf (or the installation path). The --config option allows you to directly specify the target configuration file path.

Command Function Key Flags (Options) (default) Query top N MariaDB table sizes [count] : Number of results (default 20) tables Detailed MariaDB table size query [count] : Number of results --by-month : View monthly data aggregation opensearch OpenSearch index size query --by-month : View monthly data aggregation Checks the capacity of MariaDB tables and OpenSearch indices. Two subcommands are provided: size tables and size opensearch, each supporting detailed sorting and aggregation options.
# Basic usage (check top n MariaDB tables) tkctl size [n] # Detailed MariaDB table check tkctl size tables [n] [flags] # Detailed OpenSearch index check tkctl size opensearch [flags]

# All tables tkctl size tables # Top 20 tables tkctl size tables 20 # Backward compatibility tkctl size 20 # LOG table monthly data aggregation tkctl size tables --by-month tkctl size db -m Output example: 2025-12-24 21:03:53 [INFO] ### MariaDB Table Capacity Check (00133FKFKG) ### 2025-12-24 21:03:53 [INFO] 🔎 Displaying top 20 tables. ------------------------------------------------------------------------ TABLE NAME SIZE ROWS ------------------------------------------------------------------------ TPU_ISTAT_MEDIA_EVENT_LOG 19.3 MB 14,005 TPU_INFO_DEPLOYING_DETAIL 960.0 KB 17 TPU_INFO_ENDPOINT_ISSUE_HISTORY 960.0 KB 15 TPU_ISTAT_SYS_LOG 800.0 KB 213 TPU_INFO_SCHEDULE 720.0 KB 15 TPU_ISTAT_UMS_HISTORY_SUM 720.0 KB 15 TPU_ISTAT_MEDIA_BLOCK_SUM 720.0 KB 15 TPU_ISTAT_EVENT_LOG 704.0 KB 306 TPU_ISTAT_SYSTEM_SELF_TEST_LOG 592.0 KB 441 TPU_ISTAT_AGENT_INTEGRITY_LOG 528.0 KB 95 ------------------------------------------------------------------------ 2025-12-24 21:03:53 [SUCC] Table capacity check complete. Output example (monthly aggregation): 2026-01-13 23:21:10 [INFO] Aggregating *_LOG tables by month... 2026-01-13 23:21:10 [INFO] LOG tables found: 44 ### TPU_ISTAT_MEDIA_EVENT_LOG (date column: client_dt) ---------------------------------- MONTH ROWS ---------------------------------- 2026-01 53,922 2025-12 30,653 2025-11 11,881 ---------------------------------- ### TPU_ISTAT_EVENT_LOG (date column: client_dt) ---------------------------------- MONTH ROWS ---------------------------------- 2026-01 423 2025-12 595 2025-11 164 ---------------------------------- Key features: MySQL-style tabular output format Automatic unit conversion (B/KB/MB/GB) Thousands separator formatting (14,005) Descending sort by size Dynamic column width adjustment Monthly aggregation option (-m, --by-month): Automatically detects date columns in *_LOG tables and aggregates data by month

Command Function Key Flags (Options) recommend Data-driven analysis recommendations --month : Target month for analysis (YYYY-MM) media-top Media control process/path Top N --month : Target month --limit : Number of results (default 20) --output : Save to CSV file agent-anomaly Detect agents with excessive logging --month : Target month --threshold : Threshold multiplier vs. average (default 5.0) process-top Per-process log aggregation --month : Target month --limit : Number of results db-trend DB data growth trend analysis --output : Save to CSV file Analyzes log data from MariaDB and OpenSearch for root cause diagnosis and anomaly detection. Helps identify the cause of log surges in production environments and automatically recommends items that need analysis.

Subcommand Description Data Source media-top Media control process+path Top N analysis OpenSearch agent-anomaly Detect agents with excessive logging OpenSearch process-top Per-process log aggregation Top N OpenSearch recommend Data-driven analysis recommendations MariaDB + OpenSearch db-trend DB data growth trend analysis MariaDB

Flag Short Description Default --month -m Target month for analysis (YYYY-MM) Current month --limit -n Limit number of results 20 --timeout Query timeout (seconds) 30 --output -o File path to save results (.csv, .txt) (console output)

Automatically diagnoses the data state and recommends which analyses should be performed. Provides an automated answer to the operator's question: "What should I analyze?"
